coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence sensor array based on amino acid derived carbon dots for pattern-based detection of toxic metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zhe
Xu, Chao
Lu, Yuexiang
Chen, Xiaotong
Yuan, Hang
Wei, Guoyu
Ye, Gang
Chen, Jing
coredata.dc:description: The development of convenient method for detection and identification of toxic metals ions is of great interest. In this work, we have developed a facile fluorescent sensor array based on luminescence carbon dots for detecting and differentiating a set of metal ions. Three kinds of nitrogen-doped carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid with three different amino acids (l-glycine, l-lysine and l-serine). These carbon dots had various surface states and thus exhibited diverse quenching responses to different metal ions. Six metal ions can be well distinguished at various concentrations (100, 50, 20 and 10μM). The discrimination accuracy of unknown samples was found to be 100% at a concentration as low as 10μM. In addition, the binary and ternary mixtures of three metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+) could also be well recognized with this sensor array. This method is simple, label-free, and the sensor arrays could be obtained and expanded easily with large-scale and low-cost. It will broaden the application field of carbon dots sensors and give a new direction of developing sensitive array sensing systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Sensor array
Metal ions
Amino acid
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Metal ions detection based on fluorescent CDs
Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots
Discrimination of six metal ions with same concentration
Fluorescence response of CDs to metal ions
Response fingerprint of CDs to different metal ions at 100μM
Discrimination of different metal ions with various concentrations
Discrimination of metal ion binary and ternary mixtures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) GlyCDs, (b) LysCDs, (c) SerCDs. Fluorescence spectra at excitation wavelengths from 300nm to 460nm of (d) GlyCDs, (e) LysCDs, (f) SerCDs (10μg/mL). Inset are the photographs of CDs under 365nm UV lamp.
Effect of metal ion concentrations (0–100μM) on the response (excited at 360nm) of the fluorescence sensor array.

(a) Fingerprints of six selected metal ions based on the patterns of the corresponding values of (I0 −I)/I0 (%) (excited at 360nm) obtained from GlyCDs, LysCDs and SerCDs. (b) Canonical score plot for the response patterns as obtained from LDA for six heavy metal ions at 100μM.

Fingerprints of six selected metal ions based on the patterns of the corresponding values of (I0 −I)/I0 (excited at 360nm) obtained from GlyCDs, LysCDs and SerCDs at (a) pH 3.0, (b) pH 5.0, (c) pH 7.0. Canonical score plot for the response patterns as obtained from LDA for six heavy metal ions at 100μM at (d) pH 3.0, (e) pH 5.0, (f) pH 7.0.
Hierarchical cluster analysis of fluorescence sensor array of six different metal ions at different metal ion concentrations: at (a) 100, (b) 50, (c) 20 and (d) 10μM.
Canonical score plot obtained from LDA for Cu2+, Fe3+, and Hg2+ ions (100μM) and their binary and ternary mixtures.
Fluorescent response of the sensing array against Cu2+, Fe3+, and Hg2+ ions (100μM) and their binary and ternary mixtures. (a) Fingerprints, (b) hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA).

head.ce:title: Fluorescence sensor array based on amino acid derived carbon dots for pattern-based detection of toxic metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Nuclear Energy Technology, Beijing Key Lab of Radioactive Waste Treatment, Tsinghua University, Beijing, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology
Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Nuclear Energy Technology
Beijing Key Lab of Radioactive Waste Treatment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of convenient method for detection and identification of toxic metals ions is of great interest. In this work, we have developed a facile fluorescent sensor array based on luminescence carbon dots for detecting and differentiating a set of metal ions. Three kinds of nitrogen-doped carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid with three different amino acids (l-glycine, l-lysine and l-serine). These carbon dots had various surface states and thus exhibited diverse quenching responses to different metal ions. Six metal ions can be well distinguished at various concentrations (100, 50, 20 and 10μM). The discrimination accuracy of unknown samples was found to be 100% at a concentration as low as 10μM. In addition, the binary and ternary mixtures of three metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+) could also be well recognized with this sensor array. This method is simple, label-free, and the sensor arrays could be obtained and expanded easily with large-scale and low-cost. It will broaden the application field of carbon dots sensors and give a new direction of developing sensitive array sensing systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Toxic metal ions pollution, which could arise from human activities such as mining, metal plating and industrial wastes, can cause serious damage to environment and human beings through direct toxicity and bioaccumulation [1,2]. Common laboratory methods, such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) and inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopies (ICP-MS) [3,4], can provide accurate quantitative information on metal ions, but their application is hampered by complex procedures, expensive equipment and inflexibility for on-site analysis. Therefore, the development of convenient method for detection and identification of toxic metal ions is of great interest. Fluorescence sensors, which possess high sensitivity, easy operation and low cost, offer an alternative for addressing these limitations. However, the traditional ‘lock-key’ sensing mode require design and synthesis of fluorescence molecules with high specificity for each metal ion. As the synthesis of these specific molecules is often challenging and they may only work in a certain condition, the analysis of a set of metal ions will need serval sensors to work one by one, which is costly and time-consuming [5]. Thus, it is difficult to identify a set of metal ions with one approach and could hardly deal with the mixture of different metal ions.

Array sensing strategy could give an alternative solution to these problems. In a sensor array, a library of nonspecific sensing elements could respond differentially to a given target analyte, giving it a fingerprint for pattern recognition. Several sensor array have been reported for discrimination of metal ions [3,5–9], but the sensing elements based on novel fluorescence molecules still suffer from complex synthesis and high cost. As the sensitivity and discrimination ability of a sensor array are critically dependent on the number of sensing elements, it is highly needed to find new kinds of materials, which do not need further modification/conjugation and could be large-scale prepared with various recognition properties, to act as extensible sensing elements.

Carbon dots (CDs), a class of carbon nanomaterial that have attracted tremendous attention owing to their tunable fluorescence emission properties, chemical inertness, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility, might be an excellent candidate for fluorescence array sensing of metal ions [10,11]. First, there are abundant functional groups on the surface of carbon dots, which could interact with various kinds of metal ions for nonspecific sensing [12–15]. Second, the type and amount of these functional groups could be changed simply by changing the raw materials, synthesis methods and reaction conditions. For example, the same raw materials treated by hydrothermal [16], microwave [17], microplasma [18] exhibited different response behavior to different metal ions. Third, carbon dots could be produced in large scale from a variety of raw resources with low-cost. Besides pure chemicals reagents, many natural precursors such as hair fiber [19], carbon black [20], milk [21], eggs [22], grass [23], fruits [24], which are widely distributed in nature and our daily life, could be used for carbon dots synthesis. However, up to now, there are very few report on using carbon dots for fabricating chemical sensor array.

In this paper, we have tried to prove that carbon dots could be utilized to act as extensible sensing elements for chemical sensor array. As a proof-of-concept system, we prepared three kinds of carbon dots by simply changing the amino acid type used in synthesis. Six toxic heavy metal ions, which can cause environmental and health problems, were selected as sensing targets. The carbon dots based sensing elements respond differently to the heavy metal ions. Metal ions at various concentrations (100, 50, 20, 10μM) and the mixture of two or three kinds of metal ions could be discriminated successfully by this sensor array.

Citric acid was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Amino acids including l-glycine (Gly), l-lysine (Lys) and l-serine (Ser) were obtained from Aladdin. Sodium hydroxide, nitric acid, and all the metal salts including Cr(NO3)3, Cu(NO3)2, Eu(NO3)3, Fe(NO3)3, Hg(NO3)2 and Pb(NO3)2 were bought from Beijing Chemical Works. De-ionized water was used for the preparation of all aqueous solutions. All reagents were used as received and with no further purification.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was taken on JEM-2010 (JEOL). UV–vis absorption spectra were analyzed on a Cary 6000i UV-vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies). Fluorescence spectroscopy was obtained with a FluoroMax-4 spectrophotometer. FT-IR spectra were measured using a Nicolet NEXUS 470 FT-IR spectrophotometer. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated by using 250XI spectrometer with a mono X-Ray source Al Kα excitation (1361eV). Binding energy calibration was based on C1s at 284.8eV. X-Ray Diffraction were performed with D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer.

The carbon dots were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. Briefly, citric acid (5mmol) and amino acid (including Gly, Lys, Ser) (10mmol) were dissolved in 10mL deionized water to make a clear solution. Then the solution was sealed into a 50mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180°C for 6h. After that, the reactors were cooled down naturally. The products, which are dark yellow or brown-black were dialyzed against DI-water with a membrane bag (Mw=1000Da) for 24h to remove the excess precursors. The solutions were dried at 80°C for a few hours to obtain solid products for further application. According to the carbon source, the resultant carbon dots were abbreviated as GlyCDs, LysCDs and SerCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ions detection based on fluorescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Three kinds of carbon dots solution at concentration of 10μg/mL were prepared. Then, metal ions (Cr3+, Cu2+, Eu3+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+) in water at different concentrations were added to these solutions to give final metal ions concentrations in the range of 2μM–100μM. Final volume of the solution was completed to 3mL with water. The final pH of the solution was adjusted to 5.0 with NaOH and HNO3 solutions. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of the solutions was performed on a fluorescence spectroscopy at the excitation wavelength equaling to 360nm. The data was processed using Origin and LDA in SPSS v19.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: Three kinds of carbon dots were firstly prepared by using citric acid and different amino acids as raw materials. According to the carbon source, the resultant carbon dots were abbreviated as GlyCDs, LysCDs and SerCDs. As shown in Fig. 1 , the TEM images demonstrated that all the CDs exhibited spherical structures in morphology and were well dispersed from each other. All CDs had a relatively narrow size distribution and the average diameters of GlyCDs, LysCDs and SerCDs are 2.3nm, 14.5nm and 2.4nm, respectively (see the Supporting information, Fig. S1). No obvious lattice fringes were observed on the three kinds of CDs and the XRD patterns also displayed that the CDs had no obvious lattice structure (see the Supporting information, Fig. S2).

Photoluminescent behaviors of the different amino acid generated CDs demonstrate a dependence on their precursors (Fig. 1d–f). All the CDs showed a classic excitation-dependent PL behavior. Along with the changed excitation wavelength from 300 to 460nm, the photoluminescence peaks of the CDs shifted from approximately 400 to 550nm. However, the maximum emission wavelengths were not the same, which were related to the sizes and their surface defects of the CDs. The maximum emission wavelengths of the three CDs were at 410nm (GlyCDs), 410nm (LysCDs) and 450nm (SerCDs), with the excitation wavelengths of 340nm (GlyCDs), 340nm (LysCDs) and 380nm (SerCDs), respectively. The quantum yields of three CDs were estimated to be 12.0% (GlyCDs), 10.6% (LysCDs) and 12.3% (SerCDs), by selecting quinine sulphate as a reference and 360nm as an excitation wavelength.

The surface structures of these three CDs were further explored by using UV–vis, FTIR and XPS and they showed similar characteristic but with some difference. As illustrated in Fig. S3 (see the Supporting information) GlyCDs, LysCDs showed the same absorption peak at around 330nm with different intensity, while SerCDs generated a different absorption shoulder at around 295nm, which could be ascribed to the π−π* electron transition of the conjugated CC band and the n–π* electron transition of the CO band [25]. In the FTIR analysis of three CDs, stretching vibrations of NH and CH, vibrational absorption band of CO, bending vibrations of NH, and asymmetric stretching vibrations of CNHC were all observed in three CDs with slightly wavelength shift (see the Supporting information, Fig. S4). Moreover, the XPS analysis of three CDs all exhibited three peaks at around 284, 400 and 531eV, which could attribute to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. Whereas, the peak position of the three CDs exist slightly difference (see the Supporting information, Fig. S5, Table S1). Meanwhile, the oxygen, carbon and nitrogen atom contents of different CDs derived from different amino acids were also different (see the Supporting information, Table S2). By analyzing the C1s peaks of three CDs, the graphitic or aliphatic (CC and CC), oxygenated, and nitrous carbon atoms were also discovered (see the Supporting information, Table S3). These results indicated that there were abundant of functional groups existed on the surface of the three CDs, while for CDs derived from different amino acids, the amount and the categories of function groups may vary.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of six metal ions with same concentration
Fluorescence response of CDs to metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: In our preliminary experiments, the fluorescence responses of the three CDs against six metal ions (Cr3+, Cu2+, Eu3+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+) at different concentrations between 2 and 100μM were investigated (Fig. 2 ). It was found that the emissions of the different CDs were quenched by a variety of metal ions with different degree and with the increasing concentration of metal ions the fluorescent intensity of different CDs decreased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response fingerprint of CDs to different metal ions at 100μM
ce:section.ce:para: For further investigation, the response fingerprint pattern of metal ions (100μM) on the sensor array were obtained. The signal change value was defined as (I0 −I)/I0, where I and I0 are the fluorescent intensity (440nm under excitation wavelength at 360nm) of the CDs solution in the presence and absence of the target metal ions, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 a, for the same CDs receptor, the (I0 −I)/I0 values varied with the addition of different metal ions. On the other side, for the same metal ions, the (I0 −I)/I0 values were dependent on different CDs receptors. The different affinity of metal ions with the three CDs could be explained with the difference in chelate formation capability of metal ions with functional groups (amino, carboxyl, hydroxyl etc.) of CDs derived from different amino acids.

To provide more clear evidence for this discrimination, these CDs responses are subjected to linear discriminate analysis (LDA) which is a statistic method for recognizing the linear combination of features that differentiate two or more classes of object. Five replicates were tested for each metal ion sample, and three canonical factors were generated (81.0%, 18.2%, 0.7%) that represent linear combinations of the response matrix (3 CDs×6 metal ions×5 replicates) obtained from the response patterns. The 35 training cases were separated into seven respective groups, with 100% accuracy according to the classification matrix derived from the analysis of subsets of the data sets, and the two factors that were most significant are plotted in 2D in which each point represents the response pattern for an individual metal ion sample against the sensor array (Fig. 3b). The discrimination accuracy of 35 unknown samples at 100μM was found to be 100% (see the Supporting information, Tables S4 and S5).

The effect of pH on the signal intensity (pH 3.0, 5.0, 7.0) was further tested. It was found that the response pattern of the array to six metal ions were similar under different pH value but with slight difference (Fig. 4 ). The single intensity at pH 3.0 was lower than that at pH 5.0 and pH 7.0. Under low pH (3.0), the COO− group at the surface of CDs was preferred to exist as COOH, which would affect their interaction with metal ions. While, under high pH value (7.0), some metal ions started to hydrolyze and precipitate were observed, which also might decrease the single intensity. Although the discrimination ability of this sensor array under different pH were all good enough to discriminate the samples at 100μM with 100% accuracy, the best performance was obtained at pH 5.0. Thus, pH 5.0 was chosen for further experiments.

To test the sensitivity of the sensor array, the discrimination of metal ions at lower concentrations (10μM) was also investigated. In the blind test at 10μM, we found that 35 unknown metal ion samples could be successfully identified and the discrimination accuracy was 100% (see the Supporting information, Tables S6 and S7).

Another data analysis method, hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) analysis, was also used for investigating the discrimination ability of the sensor array. HCA is a method of cluster analysis that seeks to build a hierarchy of clusters and has been used in data mining and statistics. The results of HCA analysis of the array response against metal ions at different concentrations were shown in Fig. 5 . It demonstrated that at higher metal ion concentrations (10, 20, 50 and 100μM) the analysis can successfully categorize all experimental trials into the same group for a given metal while separating the distinct metals into different categories without a mistake. At a lower concentration (2μM), the sensor array could not discriminate all the six metal ions. Hg2+ still could be identified correctly. The other five metal ions and blank sample were divided into three groups (Cu2+, Fe3+), (Cr3+, Eu3+) and (Pb2+, blank). Inside each group, the sensor array could not discriminate the samples effectively, as the discrimination accuracy was not 100% (see the Supporting information, Fig. S6). There results indicated that our sensor array could work at a concentration as low as 10μM. The sensitivity of this sensor array is comparable to recently reported arrays [6–9].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of different metal ions with various concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of sensor array to discriminate six metal ions at four different concentrations (100, 50, 20 and 10μM) was also tested. The discrimination accuracy of 125 samples (6 metal ions×4 concentrations×5 replicates+blank×5 replicates) is 98.4% (123/125), demonstrating that our sensor array could discriminate metal ions of various concentrations (see the Supporting information, Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of metal ion binary and ternary mixtures

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, we have demonstrated that carbon dots could be utilized as extensible sensing elements to fabricate chemical sensor array. By simply changing the amino acid type used in synthesis, three kinds of carbon dots with different optical properties and surface states were obtained. Sensor array based on these three CDs could be used to detect and discriminate six toxic heavy metal ions at various concentrations with a detection limit of 10μM. As hundreds of carbon dots have been reported and new kinds of carbon dots with different properties could be prepared easily with large-scale and low-cost, it is expected that a huge number of sensor array could be established for discrimination of various targets. Also, as carbon dots have multidimensional information, they could be used to fabricate high-order sensor arrays with different sensing mechanisms and multidimensional information. Our work would open a new direction of fabricating sensor array and application of carbon dots.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21390413, 51425403, 21405090 and 91426302), Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (IRT13026) and Tsinghua University Initiative Scientific Research Program (2014z22063).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.09.186.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Figures and tables showing the characterizations of three carbon dots and the array sensing of metal ions.
The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhe Wang is currently studying for doctoral degree at Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Tsinghua University, China. Her main research interest lies in the field of synthesis of functionalized carbon dots and their sensing application.

Chao Xu received his Ph.D. degree in Chemistry at Peking University (China) in 2009. Now he is an associate professor at Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Tsinghua University, China. His research interest focus on coordination chemistry and separation of metal ions.

Yuexiang Lu received his Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry at Tsinghua University (China) in 2013. Now he is an assistant professor at Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Tsinghua University, China. His research interest focus on the preparation of functional nanomaterials and their applications in optical sensors.

Xiaotong Chen received her Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry at Tsinghua University (China) in 2011. Now she is an associate professor at Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Tsinghua University, China. Her research interest focus on fluorescence sensing.
Hang Yuan received his Ph.D. degree in Chemical Engineering at Tsinghua University (China) in 2016. His research interest focus on the preparation of nanowires and their applications in biosensors.

Guoyu Wei is currently studying for doctoral degree at Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Tsinghua University, China. His research interest focus on the electrochemistry and nanomaterials.

Gang Ye received his Ph.D. degree in Chemical Engineering at Tsinghua University (China) in 2010. Now he is an associate professor at Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Tsinghua University, China. His research interest includes nanomaterials synthesis and separation of metal ions.

Jing Chen received his Ph.D. degree in Chemical Engineering at Tsinghua University (China) in 1996. Now he is a full professor at Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Tsinghua University, China. His research interest includes separation of metal ions with novel materials.
